Presenting at Microsoft MLADS 2025

# Velocity Coding presentation @ Microsoft

- [Report this article](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fpulse%2Fvelocity-coding-presentation-microsoft-ben-zo-wuioe&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=PONCHO_ARTICLE&_f=guest-reporting)

[Ben Zo](https://www.linkedin.com/in/0thernet)\
![Ben Zo](data:image/jpeg;base64,)

### Ben Zo

#### Zofounder at Zo Computer

Published Sep 25, 2025

[+ Follow](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=%2Fpulse%2Fvelocity-coding-presentation-microsoft-ben-zo-wuioe&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_publisher-author-card)

Today I gave a talk to 700 Microsoft engineers about how I code with AI.

They introduced me as the guy who spent $9k on Cursor in a month. I boiled down everything I would've wanted to know when I was just getting started. Sharing a quick rundown of my talk.

"Make it work, then make it good" – famous old adage of software engineers. Hits different when you can make a TON of code work real fast.

This is my AI coding mantra, in 3 principles. Always optimize for flow state. There's a new kind of flow state when you can iterate super fast.

Avoid brainrot coding. Many forms of this with AI. Stay laser-focused on the goal: true velocity. True velocity means you make zero mistakes.

AI coding boils down to one trick: What if you could just do one other thing in parallel, while you think hard about the main thing? Sounds simple – but code on tap is incredibly powerful.

I mostly don't use CLI agents. They're useful to me in scripts and automations. But I want MORE UI, not less of it.

I never use MCP plugins. I believe they can be useful sometimes, but personally I never want to handicap my AI with bloated context.

AI code review is dumb. Maybe it'll get better. But it's way too dumb to be useful today.

Maybe I'm just built different, but I don't believe anyone is actually effectively using parallel agents, except maybe in super sloppy situations. If you disagree, tell me your workflow!

Rules are just a small, ineffective piece of the puzzle. You want your codebase to feel like a living system, in charge of its own destiny. If your code + types + lints + comments + automated checks guide you to the right place, you're on your way to glory.

Summarizing my philosophy again. Talk to yourself. Talk to the code. Then talk to AI.

This is my workflow! I ended the talk with a live demo planning and building a quick feature. If you'd like me to give this talk to a group of people you know, DM me! Microsoft loved it :)
